coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of fluorescent carbon dots obtained from citrus x sinensis by an eco-friendly method
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zaca-Moran, O.
Díaz-Monge, F.
Rodríguez-Juárez, A.
Gómez-Muñoz, C.L.
Zaca-Moran, P.
Secundino-Sánchez, O.
Díaz-Reyes, J.
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis and characterization of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) obtained by an environmentally friendly method through carbonization, fragmentation and centrifugation of orange peel (citrus x sinensis) are reported. By thermogravimetry, 4 degradation regions associated with hemicellulose, lignin and organic matter were found. The SEM micrograph shows a rough surface morphology with flakes and granulation due to the carbonization process. X-ray diffraction (XRD) shows a hexagonal crystalline structure associated with graphitic carbon, which was confirmed by Raman scatterings, Raman spectroscopy that presented two main vibrational bands, D and G, associated with active defects in the graphitic carbon crystalline structure. The Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrograph showed CDs of size 33.65 ± 0.68n, and with High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM) an interplanar distance of 0.213 nm in the direction of the crystal planes (100) of graphitic carbon was obtained, which were confirmed by XRD and Raman spectroscopy. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy revealed the relative atomic composition and identified the strongest photoelectron transitions of carbon, sp2 (286 eV) and sp3 (285 eV), in graphitic carbon. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy of the CDs obtained from fragmentation graphitic carbon showed a band at 205 nm associated with CC bonds π-π* of CC and n-π*, as well as two bands at 276 and 328 nm associated with bond transitions. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum showed bands associated with CHs bonds as well as CC, CN bonds of the CDs. Fluorescence spectroscopy showed three emission bands at 2.43, 2.54 and 2.87 eV. The CDs showed the emission of the characteristic blue colour.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoparticles
Carbon
Fluorescent
Spectroscopy
Citrus x sinensis
CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: It illustrates the scheme for obtaining GOQDs from carbonized orange peel. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

A) thermogram of the carbonized orange peel to obtain carbon nanoparticles, b) sem micrographs of the carbonized orange peel and c) x-ray diffraction pattern of the graphitic carbon obtained from the orange peel. The inset of Fig. 2c illustrates the complete X-ray diffraction spectrum (20° ≥ 2θ ≥ 80°). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

A) xps survey spectrum of graphitic carbon obtained from orange peel, b) high-resolution spectrum of graphitic carbon allows to determine sp2 and sp3 hybridization bonds, and c) high-resolution oxygen spectrum to determine CO bonds and CO. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

A) tem image of carbon particles obtained from exfoliation, as well as its histogram to estimate the average particle size, b) tem image of graphene oxide and c) hrtem image of graphene oxide quantum dots. the histogram of particle sizes is shown in the insert of fig. a), which shows the width of the diameter distribution.

A) uv–Vis spectra of the carbonized orange peel and the CD’s and b) graph of the energy calculation of the band gap of the carbon nanoparticles. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
It illustrates the FTIR spectra: a) orange peel and b) CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

It shows the Raman spectra of a) carbonized orange peel and b) CDs obtained of orange peel. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
It presents fluorescence spectrum of the CD’s.
It illustrates the of functional group abundance for carbon and oxygen.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of fluorescent carbon dots obtained from citrus x sinensis by an eco-friendly method

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Depto. de Física, CINVESTAV-IPN. Av. Instituto Politécnico Nacional 2508. San Pedro Zacatenco, Gustavo A. Madero, Ciudad de México, 07360. México

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis and characterization of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) obtained by an environmentally friendly method through carbonization, fragmentation and centrifugation of orange peel (citrus x sinensis) are reported. By thermogravimetry, 4 degradation regions associated with hemicellulose, lignin and organic matter were found. The SEM micrograph shows a rough surface morphology with flakes and granulation due to the carbonization process. X-ray diffraction (XRD) shows a hexagonal crystalline structure associated with graphitic carbon, which was confirmed by Raman scatterings, Raman spectroscopy that presented two main vibrational bands, D and G, associated with active defects in the graphitic carbon crystalline structure. The Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrograph showed CDs of size 33.65 ± 0.68n, and with High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM) an interplanar distance of 0.213 nm in the direction of the crystal planes (100) of graphitic carbon was obtained, which were confirmed by XRD and Raman spectroscopy. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy revealed the relative atomic composition and identified the strongest photoelectron transitions of carbon, sp2 (286 eV) and sp3 (285 eV), in graphitic carbon. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy of the CDs obtained from fragmentation graphitic carbon showed a band at 205 nm associated with CC bonds π-π* of CC and n-π*, as well as two bands at 276 and 328 nm associated with bond transitions. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum showed bands associated with CHs bonds as well as CC, CN bonds of the CDs. Fluorescence spectroscopy showed three emission bands at 2.43, 2.54 and 2.87 eV. The CDs showed the emission of the characteristic blue colour.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the World Bank, 2010 million tons of urban solid waste are generated worldwide, of which 37 % is destined for open landfills, 35 % for recycling, 19 % for composting and 11 % for incineration [1]. Taking advantage of the generation of these organic wastes is a national priority for any country, giving them added value with biotechnological applications such as carbon-based fluorescent biomarkers. This carbon-based fluorescent nanoparticles (NP's) have been of particular interest because of they have been potentially applied in all areas of engineering and technology, with a strong impact in the area of biomedicine [2], in particular for diagnosis and analysis by bioimaging [3]. This type of carbon nanoparticles present fluorescence associated with both the nanometric size and the present functional groups, which makes them ideal for applications in the biomedical area, since they are in the order of size of proteins and nucleic acids, for mention a few [4,5]. Currently there is a wide variety of techniques for obtaining fluorescent nanoparticles that use expensive technological resources and excessive consumption of resources [6] such as high pressure, high temperature, modifying the properties of fluorescence [7]. One of these methods is the high temperature organometallic route [8]. This method uses hot injection of hexadecyl phosphonic acid as a surfactant [9]. There are also techniques for the synthesis of nanoparticles with potentially harmful precursors for biomedical applications to be used in the human body [4] based on semiconductor compounds of elements from the periodic table, particularly from groups II-VI or III-V, with dimensions smaller than their Bohr excitonic radii (<6 nm) [10]. Currently there is a great challenge in obtaining fluorescent nanoparticles using different synthesis routes without using potentially harmful precursors [8].

For example, Adhimoorthy et al. [11] performed a simple and facile one-pot synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from waste orange peels using hydrothermal carbonization method at mild temperature (180 °C). The carbon was amorphous in nature and the polyaromatic hydrocarbon groups included a large amount of oxygen functional groups. These were implemented for the degradation of naphthol blue-black azo dye under UV irradiation, demonstrating the photocatalytic activity.

On the other hand, Hu et al. [12] used carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared by a one-step eco-friendly approach for sensitive and selective assay of Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli). CQDs from orange peel were obtained by microwave-assisted method, which showed strong green fluorescence under an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The authors fabricated a fluorescent probe (CQD-MNP) for E. coli based on aptamer-labelled CQDs (aptamer-CQD) and magnetic nanoparticles with complementary DNA (cDNA-MNP). The fluorescence intensity of CQD-MNPs was reduced by the addition of E. coli. The linearity between fluorescence intensity and E. coli concentration was used to develop a fluorescent method with a detection range of 500–106 CFU/ml and a detection limit of 487 CFU/ml. This fluorescent probe has great potential for ensuring food quality and safety [12].

Obtaining fluorescent nanoparticles is the main objective in this work, via green synthesis using an organic residue for its use in biotechnological areas, in addition to the ease of obtaining it together with its chemical composition, making them ideal to be implemented in biotechnological applications [13–15].

In this research, organic waste such as orange peel is used to give it added value by obtaining carbon dots (CDs) for implementation as biomarkers or contrast agents with high bio-compatibility with humans, due to which are mainly made of carbon presenting a high fluorescence, which are obtained by a carbonization and fragmentation process, a relatively easy and environmentally friendly method. As such, the technique is not new, but it is economical and takes advantage of agroindustrial waste to obtain CDs and give them added value, which are used as fluorescent biomarkers [14,15].

The chemical materials used to obtain the fluorescent CDs were analytical grade, 2-propanol (C3H8O) with a purity of 99.99 % from Sigma Aldrich, nitrogen, as well as deionized water (>18 MΩ cm−1) that was used for wash the laboratory equipment in the manufacture of the CDs obtained by fragmentation the carbonized orange peel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots from carbonized orange peel
ce:section.ce:para: The orange peel was obtained from the citrus variety Citrus x sinensis, washed with deionized water and subsequently dehydrated. The dehydrated peel was carbonized at 1100 °C in a BL Barnstead Thermolyne 1300 muffle, using a cylindrical reactor in a controlled nitrogen atmosphere. 50 mg of the carbonized peel was taken and placed in 10 ml of isopropyl alcohol. For the fragmentation process, a BRANSON 3800 ultrasonic cuvette was used for 3 h at a frequency of 40 kHz, and then the solution was centrifuged at 15,000 revolutions per minute (rpm) for 15 min, obtaining a colloidal solution that was transparent to visible light as seen in Fig. 1 , in relation to the carbon yield, 25–30 % is obtained, with respect to CDs this is 2.3 µg/ml. In order to perform fluorescence spectroscopy, 2 ml of CDs in isopropyl alcohol were taken, which were obtained from carbon fragmentation and centrifugation [16,17].

The characterization by thermogravimetry (TGA) was carried out in a TA instruments Thermal Analyzer SDT Q600 equipment, in a nitrogen atmosphere (50 ml/min) with a heating ramp of 10 °C/min in an operating range of room temperature to 1100 °C. The surface morphology of the graphitic carbon was characterized in a scanning electron microscope (SEM) model TESCAN VEGA TS 5136SB), with a resolution of 3 nm in high vacuum with secondary and backscattered detectors, with a magnification range of 4–500000 × and an acceleration voltage of 0.5–30 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was obtained in a Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer, with a CuKα (1.54 Å) radiation source. The chemical analysis was performed by X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) with a Thermo Scientific system in ultrahigh vacuum condition (Pressure of 1 × 10-9 Torr) a monochromated AlKα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) is employed to generate core excitations. The binding energy of all elements was calibrated using the CC bonding in the C 1s peak (284.8 eV). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs were obtained on a Tecnai G2 T20 TEM, operating at 300 kV. HRTEM studies were carried out in a JEOL JEM200 of 80–200 kV; the obtained image is recorded with a CCD camera in real time. The Gatan Digital Micrograph software was used for the analysis of HRTEM images. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum was obtained using a THERMO Scientific Model Evolution 600 equipment, with a quartz cell with a 1 cm path length. The FT-IR spectra were recorded using a BRUKER model Vertex spectrophotometer model Vertex, in the 400–4000 cm−1 wavelength region, with ±4 cm−1 error rate, the measurement was performed in ATR mode (Attenuated Total Reflectance) with a time integration of 120 scans in the sample and 60 scans for the baseline using the ATR crystal as the baseline, data acquisition was performed with OPUS 6.0 software. Raman scattering was measured in a THERMO SCIENTIFIC model DXR Smart Raman spectrophotometer with an operating range of 50–3350 cm−1 with a 780 nm infrared laser and 12 mW output power with a thermoelectrically cooled CCD detector. The fluorescence spectrum was obtained using an Ocean Optics USB 4000 spectrophotometer coupled to an optical fibre with a 365 nm LED excitation source and a Toshiba TCD1304 CCD detector, with a detection range of 200–1100 nm.

The curves obtained by the TGA analyses show 4 regions of pyrolytic degradation of the carbonised orange peel, showing both the dehydration of the sample and the degradation of the organic material. The volatilization of terpenes, oils and pigments occurs in the temperature range of 40–170 °C, the degradation of hemicellulose occurs in the range of 170–250 °C, between 270 and 450 °C the degradation and disintegration of lignin, so weight loss before 500 °C is associated with dehydration, as well as volatile organic material [18,19]. From 500–900 °C, the cyclization process begins, which are the chemical reactions that produce the closure of molecular structures to form carbon rings, favouring dehydrogenation, increasing aromaticity, which accelerates cyclization to subsequently obtain graphitic carbon, with a yield of ∼19.84 % [20]. The graph illustrated in Fig. 2 a shows the analysis of the pyrolysis corresponding to the orange peel, observing the 4 main degradation peaks before biomass carbonization.

Fig. 2b illustrates the SEM micrograph of the sample, in which a rough surface morphology obtained from the carbonized orange peel is observed with flakes with cavities associated with the pores generated during the carbonization process [21].

Fig. 2c illustrates the X-ray diffraction pattern the graphitic carbon obtained from the orange peel, which shows diffraction peaks at
2 θ = 26
and
2 θ =
43 °
, associated with the directions of the (002) and (100) crystalline planes of graphitic carbon [22–27]. As well as other diffraction peaks at
2 θ =
47 ,
48
and
54 °
associated with directions (101), (102) and (004) crystalline planes, respectively (JCPDS 41-1484, JCPDS 075-1621) [25]. In addition, it has been reported that the diffraction peak at
2 θ =
29 °
corresponds to the carbon coming from the orange peel [26–29]. The interplanar distance of 2.02 \AA calculated at
2 θ =
43 °
peak corresponds to the (100) crystalline direction of the graphitic carbon, close to the reported value of 2.1319 \AA [23], associated with the hexagonal crystalline phase. It estimated lattice constants, hexagonal crystalline phase, a = 2.404 Å and c = 6.765 Å that are close to the values reported for a = 2.4617 Å and c = 6.7106 Å, which confirms that the graphitic carbon particles have a hexagonal crystalline structure [23]. The inset of Fig. 2c illustrates the complete X-ray diffraction spectrum (20° ≥ 2θ ≥ 80°), which shows that 2θ ≥ 60° there are no well-resolved diffraction peaks with significant intensities. For this reason, only the first part of the XRD spectrum was emphasized. Using the Scherrer equation, the particle size can be estimated, obtaining a value of 29.71 nm. In the literature it is mentioned that the X-ray diffraction patterns present 2 peaks around 26 and 43° that are associated with the directions (002) and (100), an interplanar distance of 2.082 Å in the direction (100) of the hexagonal lattice with lattice constants of values a = 2.404 Å and c = 6.765 Å, which are related to graphite oxide [23] .

Using XPS spectroscopy, the chemical states of various elements and the presence of functional groups were evaluated, the energies belonging to the sp2 and sp3 hybridizations are found, since in the literature it is reported that the values of these energies are of greater importance to demonstrate their respective hybridizations, which are presented through the XPS study spectrum (Fig. 3 a) of graphitic carbon showing the binding energies belonging to C 1 (285 eV) and O 1 (531 eV). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of carbon (Fig. 3b) shows the deconvolution of the binding energies associated with the sp2 (286.5 eV) and sp3 (285 eV) hybridizations. Additionally, showing the energies of the CO bonds (287.5 eV) and COOH groups (293 eV). Fig. 3c shows the deconvolution of the XPS spectrum of oxygen, which presents 2 binding energies, one associated with CO (533 eV) and the other associated with CO (534 eV) [30]. Table 1 shows the abundance of functional groups for carbon and oxygen [31].

After the fragmentation of the graphitic carbon obtained from the carbonised orange peel, Fig. 4 a illustrates the TEM image that shows almost spherical particles with an average size of 33.65 ± 0.68 nm, a value obtained from the Gaussian distribution as is observed in the inset of Fig. 4a. From the normal distribution, the coefficient of variation was estimated with a value of 25.81 %, marking a slight dispersion in sizes with an estimated width of the diameter distribution of 21.6 nm, and a standard deviation of σ = ±8.68 nm. Fig. 4b illustrates a TEM image showing showing a larger area. The HRTEM microscopy image shows almost spherical nanoparticles are observed, which presents the lattice fringes with an interplanar distance of 2.13 \AA in the crystallographic direction (100), this result suggests that the fragmentation graphitic carbon nanoparticles become CDs, corresponding to a hexagonal crystalline structure, as is seen in Fig. 4b [21,32]. This is because of in the exfoliation process and in the centrifugation process, according to the chosen rpm, a certain type of particle size is selected, which influences the colour of the emission, reasons why which sizes of 33.65 nm are observed with similar morphology between them.

As is observed in Fig. 5 a, the absorption spectrum of the graphitic carbon shows the absorption band at 204 nm, which is associated with the CC bonds of the aromatic rings [33]; as well as the absorption spectrum of the CDs with absorption bands at 276 and 328 nm, associated with the transitions of the π-π* bonds of CC and n-π* of the CO/CN bonds, respectively, these bands are strongly related to CDs [34,35]. The direct band gap energy (Eg ) was determined using the Tauc's model of direct transitions with the optical absorption coefficient (α) obtained from optical transmittance spectrum
α = [ l o g ( 100 / T o ) ] / [ τ l o g e ]
, two band gap energies of 1.58 and 3.40 eV were obtained, as illustrated in Fig. 5b [36–38].

The previously dehydrated orange peel was characterized by FTIR spectroscopy before carbonization, which showed a wide and intense band at 3300 cm−1 related to OH grups of alcohol and carboxylic acids, as well as two narrow bands at 2920 and 2840 cm−1 belonging to the aliphatic acids groups (CH, CH2 and CH3). The vibrational bands between 1200 and 1270, 1627 and 1735 cm−1 are associated with the group of amides, a secondary band at 1516 cm−1 is associated with amide II, as well as NH, CN bonds. In addition to ester groups CO, CN, CC, in the region of 900–1200 cm−1 there are bands associated with CC, CO, COC and COH groups and also bonds associated with citric acid are observed (3400, 2997,1707,1391 and 933 cm−1 as can be seen in Fig. 6 a [39–42]. The infrared spectrum of the CDs shows bands belonging to CH bonds (2920-2840 cm−1), as well as CO amide bonds (1735-1620 cm−1), as well as bonds at 1380 cm−1 belonging to CN bonds, OH bonds are not observed due to the carbonization process as can be seen in Fig. 6b, because during carbonization eliminates all organic material, mainly carbon and hydrogen bonds predominating, which give rise to sp, sp2 and sp3 hybridizations and are reflected in the band intensity 2920-2840 cm−1 [43], chemical bonds such as CO CC and CO-H groups are corroborated by XPS spectroscopy.

Fig. 7 a shows the Raman spectrum of graphitic carbon, where two predominant vibrational bands are observed at 1307 and 1588 cm−1 assigned to the D and G phononic modes, these bands are also associated with active structural defects of graphitic carbon [44,45]. The G band is related to the stretching shifts of the bonds of the pairs of carbon atoms, as well as to the vibrations in the plane for sp2, which is doubly degenerate of the transverse optical (TO) and optical longitudinal (LO) phononic active modes; while the vibrational band D is the phononic band associated with the stretching shifts of the carbon rings, whose vibrational frequencies were found by deconvolution of the Raman spectra. Additionally, the D* band is related to the vibrations corresponding to sp2 and sp3, in relation to the D’’ band, this is associated with impurities in interstitial sites of the CDs lattice, as well as oxygen-related defects [44–46]. The intensities of the D and G vibrational bands are related to the crystalline quality and the density of active defects in the CDs structure. In general, it is known that the D vibrational band is related to the disorder in the CDs structural lattice and the G band with the vibrations of the plane of the carbon atoms due to sp2 hybridizations, as well as the vibrations of the phononic lattice. In Fig. 7b an increase in the G band is observed, indicating an increase in the degree of crystallinity in the CDs as reported by Cançado et al. [47] and Bruna et al [48] who related these intensities, using the
I D
/
I G
ratio, with the density of active Raman defects
(
n D
)
by means of the following equation for λ = 780 nm:
n D
cm
- 2
= 2.16 ×
10
11
I D
I G
As has been reported, for a honeycomb lattice of graphene oxide layer, the number of carbon atoms is calculated as
n C
= 3.82 ×
10
15
cm
- 2
[48] and the concentration of Raman active defects in parts per million (ppm) can be determined by the ratio
n D
/
n C
. In order to the Raman spectra of CDs obtained from the carbonized orange peel, 75.6 ppm was obtained, Fig. 7a, while for the CDs, a defect density of 56.2 ppm was obtained, see Fig. 7b. This is because D* and D” decrease when the crystallinity increases, these vibrational bands are obtained from the deconvolution of the D and G bands [49].

On the other hand, the grain size of CDs (La ) can be obtained from the intensity ratio of between the D and G bands through the equation:
L a
n m
=
560
E
laser
4
I D
I G
- 1
where Elaser is the excitation laser energy used in the experiments expressed in electron volts, and ID and IG are the intensities of the D and G bands, respectively [50–52]. Expressing the laser line wavelength (λlaser) in nm units, equation can be rewritten as:
L a
n m
=
2.3702 ×
10
- 10
λ
laser
4
I D
I G
- 1
Using the D and G intensities of the Raman spectra presented in Fig. 7, the particle sizes for carbonized orange peel and CDs are obtained, which are 34.20 and 35.14 nm, respectively. The estimated value of the particle size is in agreement with the values estimated by X-ray diffraction and by TEM. The observed differences can be attributed to the approximations used.

The CDs were excited with a UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm in isopropyl alcohol, which showed a characteristic blue colour of the fluorescence of the CDs. In the fluorescence spectrum, 3 emission bands are observed centred at 2.43, 2.54 and 2.87 eV. These are due to the fact that the CDs are rich in oxygen, as well as π-π* and n-π* bonds from the sp2 hybridizations and transitions of functional groups on the surface of the particles. These emission bands as well as the excitation band are shown in Fig. 8
[36,53,54]. In addition to using only orange peel, they were tested with other agro-industrial waste such as coffee beans and wheat peel. However, those that present the most fluorescence are the CDs from the orange peel, which is affected by the size of the carbon nanoparticle, as well as the functional groups since by varying the time and centrifugation speeds, that a centrifuge at low rpm (5000 rpm), an emission is observed in the red (675 nm), while a centrifugation at higher rpm (15000 rpm) presents a blue emission (439 nm). As shown in Fig. 1, these carbon particles show excellent fluorescence with emission in the blue, ideal for their implementation in areas of health sciences as biomarkers of pathogenic agents and/or cancer cells.

This optical fluorescence response of CDs is attributed to intrinsic states formed by sp2 carbon subdomains and extrinsic states created by various oxygen functional groups. In addition, the fluorescence behaviour shows different trends depending on the size and quantity of oxygen functional groups. Furthermore, the alternating fluorescence behaviour forms localized sp2 carbon subdomains with intrinsic emission through the reduction of oxygen functional groups in the CDs, which have fluorescence originating from the energy states of various oxygen functional groups [55].

The orange peel samples were completely carbonized from 700 °C, since no organic material was observed because the graph no longer shows variations in relation to weight after this temperature according to the TGA graphs. These carbonized samples presented a rough and flaky surface characteristic of carbonized materials. The XRD diffractogram showed two peaks of graphitic carbon (002) and (100) with an interplanar distance of 2.07 Å of a hexagonal crystal structure. By XPS, a 67 % abundance was found for bonds associated with COOH bonds in relation to carbon and a 59.77 % abundance in CO bonds in relation to oxygen, these bonds are also present in FTIR spectra. The defect density for graphitic carbon was 75.6 ppm while for CDs it was 56.2 ppm, obtained by Raman scattering. The samples presented 3 emissions, with the emission at 2.87 eV predominating, associated with the characteristic blue of CDs. This could be due to oxygen-rich CDs as well as to the intrinsic states of sp2 and sp3 hybridizations, which are corroborated by XPS and FTIR. This ecological synthesis method through the fragmentation process makes it ideal for obtaining fluorescent CDs with potential as image contrast agents or biomarkers of pathogens such as Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, to name a few, as well as tumour biomarkers. These results confirm that the carbonization of agro-industrial waste could be implemented as pathogen biomarkers or used for diagnostic imaging due to its high fluorescence.

O. Zaca Moran: Software, Resources. F. Díaz Monge: Data curation. A. Rodríguez Juárez: Investigation, Formal analysis. C.L. Gómez Muñoz: Formal analysis, Data curation. P. Zaca Moran: Software, Resources. O. Secundino-Sánchez: Methodology, Investigation. J. Díaz-Reyes: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support is gratefully acknowledged from Secretaría de Investigación y Posgrado, Instituto Politécnico Nacional (project SIP20240719) as well as the CONAHCYT (project CF-2023-G-1080).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: A. Anda-Trasviña, E. García-Galindo, A. Z. Peña-Castañón, J. V. Seminario-Peña, A. Nieto-Garibay. Residuos orgánicos: ¿basura o recurso? Recursos Naturales y Sociedad 7 (3) (2021) 19-42. 10.18846/renaysoc.2021.07.07.03.0004.
